message:
test(frankenterm-core): add TOON-format envelope coverage to MCP conformance suite + document patterns.rs fail-fast expects

Two small, orthogonal changes bundled together because they share the
same file hierarchy and both landed as part of the ongoing conformance
hardening pass.

**1. MCP conformance coverage for TOON response envelopes.**

`mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs` previously exercised only the default
JSON envelope path for every tool under test. The FrankenTerm MCP
server also accepts `format: "toon"` (TOON = the toon-rust serializer
used for agent-friendly, diff-stable payloads), but there was no
structural proof that the TOON serialization of a successful response
matches the JSON serialization once both are canonicalized. This adds
that proof.

Helpers added (in the test module):

- `parse_toon_value(text)` — decodes a TOON text payload via
  `toon_rust::try_decode`, pipes it through
  `toon_rust::cli::json_stringify::json_stringify_lines`, and parses
  the result as `serde_json::Value`. Two-stage because TOON's decoded
  form is not directly interchangeable with `serde_json::Value`; going
  through the JSON stringifier is the canonical bridge.
- `parse_tool_envelope_with_format(contents, format)` — dispatches on
  format string (`"toon"` vs anything-else) so existing JSON-path tests
  can keep using `parse_tool_envelope` unchanged.
- `assert_invalid_format_envelope_shape(envelope)` — asserts that a
  request with `format: "yaml"` produces the standard error envelope
  shape with `error_code = "FT-MCP-0001"`, an error message containing
  `"Invalid format 'yaml'"`, and a hint that names both supported
  formats (`"json"` and `"toon"`).
- `canonical_value(value)` — clones a `Value` and runs the existing
  `canonicalize(&mut cloned)` helper, so we have a one-liner for
  structural equality testing.
- `assert_toon_success_matches_json(tool_name, json_envelope,
  toon_envelope)` — asserts both envelopes pass the success shape
  check and that their canonicalized forms are byte-identical. The
  panic message names the offending tool so bisection is easy when a
  single tool's TOON serializer drifts.

The `wa.search` conformance test (shown in this diff) is updated to
drive the TOON format through the same capture path it already uses
for JSON, then run both through the new equality helper. Subsequent
tool suites will follow the same pattern; this commit is the first
pair-wise assertion.

**2. Document why three `PatternEngine` .expect()s are intentional.**

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs` has three `.expect(...)`
sites that panic on errors from code paths that are invariants of the
builtin pattern library. Each panic has been around for a while, but
the intent was implicit, which makes them a magnet for well-meaning
refactors that convert `expect` into `?` on the theory that "panics
are bad". That would be a regression: silently returning a degraded
PatternEngine hides missing detections, and the whole point of these
sites is that we'd rather abort startup than ship a matcher set that
can't match.

Three single-line comments added (comment-only change, no behavior
delta):

- `PatternEngine::new()` — builtin packs are repo-owned defaults; an
  invalid builtin must abort startup, not degrade silently.
- `PatternEngine::with_packs()` sibling ctor — the `OnceLock` was just
  created locally on the preceding line, so a second initialization
  would indicate an internal logic regression.
- `PatternEngine::index()` lazy init — if validated built-in rules
  cannot compile at runtime, the shipped matcher set is corrupted;
  continuing would hide missing detections rather than surface the
  bug.
